data.title: Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Week 237: $23,700 Allocated, $2,573.68 In Projected Dividends
data.description:
The markets rallied again heading into the FOMC meeting on 9/17/25 as the S&P 500 increased by 1.59% and the Nasdaq climbed 2.03%. We received several economic indicators that helped the stock market trend higher this week. Headline PPI declined -0.1% MoM, while Core PPI also fell by -0.1%. CPI increased 0.4%, which was in line with expectations. The initial jobless claims increased 27,000 to 263,000, which exceeded the expected 235,000, which was the highest level since October 2021. We also received a revision to GDP as it was revised upward to +3.3% annualized from 3%. While CPI and jobless claims increasing isn’t necessarily a good thing, it pretty much solidified a rate cut (by 1/4%-point to 4% to 4‑1/4%). The Fed isn’t in a position to continue holding rates where they are, as unemployment has continued higher, and the only way to achieve both sides of its dual mandate is by creating a less restrictive economic environment that encourages entrepreneurship and incentivizes businesses to borrow capital to expand. I’m still very bullish on how the remainder of the year will play out, and I believe I have built a portfolio that can do well in any market cycle while thriving in a lower rate environment. I will be looking forward to next week’s meeting and will try to add capital to some more positions that I believe will do well in the future.

This was another positive week as new records were made. After allocating $23,700 to the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio, the account balance finished week 237 at $31,822.39. The account's profitability increased by 2.75% ($217.48) to $8,122.39, which is a return on invested capital of 34.27%. This week, I added to my positions in NNN REIT (NNN), Bristol Myers (BMY), and Starwood Property Trust (STWD). The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $14.68 of dividend income in week 237. The combination of reinvesting the generated dividend income and making these investments increased the forward projected annualized dividend income by $7.53 (0.29%) to $2,573.68. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio has generated $4,785.54 of income since its inception, which is 20.19% of the total capital I have allocated toward the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio is at all-time highs and generating more dividend income than I expected it would. It’s going to be interesting to see how the remainder of the year plays out, but I think that there could be close to $2,750 of forward dividend income being generated by the time 2025 comes to an end.

The Overall Performance of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio

I don’t expect every week to be like this, but the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio has been doing quite well. Eventually it will experience another downturn, but just as it has in the past, I believe any downside risk that may be lurking around the corner will be mitigated through its level of diversification. I started this portfolio with $100 and added $100 per week toward income-producing investments. I have been documenting starting an income-producing portfolio from the ground up on a weekly budget. My investment goals were to generate recurring income while mitigating downside risk. Capital appreciation has been a secondary goal, and things are going quite well. Currently the portfolio is up $8,122.39 on invested capital (34.27%) and is generating $2,573.68 in forward dividend income, which is a portfolio yield of 8.09%. I am excited to see how the next several years unfold, as there will be a tremendous amount of capital being reinvested back into the portfolio along with the weekly allocations.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Dividend Section

Here's how much dividend income is generated per investment basket:

  • ETFs $909.21 (35.33%)
  • Equities $585.28 (22.74%)
  • CEFs $414.06 (16.09%)
  • REITs $412.09 (16.01%)
  • BDCs $253.03 (9.83%)

Investors utilize dividends to supplement their income and live off them. I'm building a dividend portfolio for myself 30 years into the future. In 2022, I collected $507.80 in dividend income from 533 dividends. In 2023, I collected $978.11 in dividend income from 660 dividends. 2024 was a great year, as the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $1,623.14 in dividend income from 709 dividends. After 37 weeks in 2025, $1,605.86 of dividend income was produced, which is 98.94% of the total income from 2024. There were 538 positions that paid a dividend, which is 75.88% of the dividends generated in 2024.

There was $14.68 of dividend income generated this week, which brought the average amount of dividend income I have generated on a weekly basis to $43.40. This is an increase of $12.19 (41.60%) YoY, and there have been 3 weeks that exceeded $100, unlike in 2024, when the largest week was $89.13. I’m really excited for the rest of September to unfold, as the YoY data is showing me a huge trend going up and to the right with dividend income. Over the next several years, I should be able to exceed $5,000 in forward dividend income in this account and basically double my annualized allocation to the portfolio.

After the first 2 weeks in September, I have generated $75.22 in dividend income, and I am looking forward to seeing how September trends. I have only exceeded $200 twice in 2025, which is also surprising, but there were also 3 months when I generated over $190 of dividend income. When I look at the 2024 trend compared to 2025, I think I am going to exceed $200 in September and from here on out. I don’t think July will be my largest month of dividend income, and based on the trends, I think I can exceed $200 in one of the months during Q4. I am excited to see how the chart below finishes the year, as I should be positioning myself to generate close to $2,700 of forward dividend income walking into 2026.

My long-term goal is to have 75% or more of the portfolio generate at least 1 share per year from their dividend income. There are currently 42 positions accomplishing this, which is emphasized by the green section of the table below. There is $450.20 of forward dividend income being generated from the new dividends being generated from all the positions generating at least 1 share from their dividends annually. Many positions are still lagging, but I am making a strong effort allocating capital toward them so I can get at least 50% of the portfolio to accomplish this goal by the end of the year. My fixation on this is because it will allow me to advance the compounding impacts from the dividend snowball, and it also makes sure that I am not investing in the same positions each week.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Composition

ETFs are trending toward 20% again, and I am still adding to REITs as they have exceeded 19% once again. It’s difficult for me to stay away from these sectors, as I believe that technology is going to heat up throughout earnings season and that there are still a lot of deals in the REIT segment. Individual equities now represent 39.09% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio while generating 22.74% of the dividend income. REITs, ETFs, CEFs, and BDCs make up 60.91% of the portfolio and generate 77.26% of the forward income. I am working on getting individual equities to represent more of the portfolio. The long-term goal is to have the sectors be close to equal weight, but that may not happen for 5–10 years at this rate, if ever. I have been adding energy companies as I believe there is a big opportunity there, and I will probably continue allocating capital toward energy positions as time progresses.

Altria Group (MO) is still above 4.5% of the portfolio, which I am still shocked about. I am happy that Enbridge (ENB) is almost near 3% and that the top 20 positions are rounding out. One of the keys to this portfolio’s success has been the level of diversification. Eventually, the top 3 positions will get smaller, and I will feel more comfortable allocating capital toward them. I am expecting the top 20 positions to change a bit next year, and I am hoping that I can get all the positions a bit more even in the future. While I have allowed myself a 5% maximum, I really want to have a well-rounded portfolio that can mitigate risk well.

I added to STWD this week, and the BlackRock Science and Technology Trust has been replaced by SL Green (SLG) in the top 10 holdings. My allocation to the top 10 positions is $6,095.17, and they finished the week with a value of $8,620.55. This is an ROI of $2,525.38 (41.43%). There has been $1,092.25 of dividends generated and reinvested, which is 17.92% of the initial investment into these positions. I'm now projecting that $575.26 of dividend income will be generated from the top 10 holdings, which have a forward yield of 9.44%. These positions represent 27.09% of the portfolio, while their forward dividend income is projected to generate 22.35% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio's annualized income.

Week 237 Additions

This week, I added to my positions in NNN REIT, Bristol Myers, and Starwood Property Trust.

NNN REIT

  • Heading into rate cuts, I wanted to add another share of NNN to the portfolio
  • NNN has grown its dividend for 34 consecutive years and has a 5-year growth rate of 2.53%
  • NNN’s dividend yield is 5.64%, which exceeds the risk-free rate of return
  • I believe that NNN is positioned to benefit from rate cuts as their buildings could be worth more valuable which would increase their book value and share price

Bristol Myers

  • BMY is deeply undervalued, trading under 10x earnings, with a 5.35% dividend yield and a robust share repurchase program
  • The company is executing a successful transition from legacy drugs to a growth portfolio, highlighted by strong Q2 results and strategic partnerships
  • I think that BMY’s growth portfolio will continue to offset its legacy portfolio, and the negativity is overdone

Starwood Property Trust

  • I just wrote an article on STWD that outlined my bull thesis (can be read here)
  • I wanted to add another share of STWD before the FOMC meeting because I believe the assets on its book will become more valuable
  • STWD also has a strong dividend that has been paid at the same rate for more than a decade
  • STWD has the ability to increase its loan originations in a lower rate environment and drive earnings higher.

Week 238 Gameplan

Next week I will probably look at add another share of NNN and VICI Properties (VICI).

Conclusion

We’re heading into rate cuts, and I am getting excited. I think the Fed is going to cut by 25 bps and continue cutting by 25 bps at the next 2 meetings to close out 2025. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio is positioned well for a lower rate environment, as there are many positions that should theoretically get a boost from lower rates. When I look at the chart below, the forward dividend income is making an S-curve, and the powers of compounding will only intensify as more dividend income is generated and then reinvested. Things are going well, and I am up 34.27% ($8,122.39) on invested capital, and the portfolio is now producing $2,573.68 in forward dividend income. When I think about the future, this portfolio could easily offset my living expenses when I decide to retire. I can’t wait to see what the remainder of 2025 looks like and how the portfolio does in 2026. Please leave all of your thoughts in the comment section below, as I enjoy interacting with everyone.

data.title: PayPal's Google Deal Fuels A Breakout
data.description:
Investment Thesis

During one of my previous exploratory research sessions, I came across an intriguing blog post from this large social media company that observed people were increasingly embracing shopping via chat. That was one of the earliest mentions of “conversational commerce.”

Over time, these social shopping behaviors appeared to evolve, through the pandemic, into social/conversational commerce, allowing people to chat with others and buy merchandise from within the chat window.

However, in this age of AI, the surge in popularity of AI agents is quickly creating a new breakout category of e-commerce, with agentic AI spearheading a new wave of growth for social/conversational commerce.

And yesterday’s partnership between Alphabet’s (GOOG) Google and PayPal (NASDAQ:PYPL) is likely going to have big implications for the growth trajectory of the digital payments giant. The deal with Google solidifies PayPal’s goals to achieve the reality of its long-term 10% growth targets, setting up the shares for a very likely breakout.

I am reiterating my Buy recommendation on PayPal and upgrading my PT on the stock.

Breaking Down PayPal’s Deal With Google

I will start with an admission about PayPal. As an existing investor in PayPal, I have been marginally disappointed with the company’s outlook this year because I was expecting a quicker acceleration path versus what the company had shared in their 2025 Investor Day at the start of this year.

I have been bullish on PayPal, and I probably got ahead of myself but that does not take away the sharp focus that PayPal’s new management has been demonstrating and executing on to return the company to its innovation-led former glory.

PayPal’s recent deal with Google is just one of the many endeavors the company is engaging in to rapidly embrace fast-growing e-commerce market categories. Broadly speaking, PayPal’s deal with Google involves some gives and takes. Alphabet CEO’s take on the Google/PayPal deal accurately summed this up:

Through this partnership, PayPal will use our industry-leading AI to enhance services and security, and we will more deeply integrate PayPal's innovative payment capabilities for a better experience across Google products and platforms.

So, what PayPal gives to Google is possibly awarding the search giant a multi-year GCP (Google Cloud Platform) contract where PayPal migrates, either a part or all, its backend tech stack onto GCP for PayPal “to enhance [its] services and security.”

In return, what PayPal gets from Google, is access to the network of +billions of users, including those +2B MAUs in Google Gemini, furthering PayPal’s vision of servicing a fast-growing category of the e-commerce market that is using agentic AI to shop.

In addition, PayPal also gets its branded checkout and SMB payment solutions embedded across all of Google’s customer and enterprise checkout flows.

Implications Of The Google Deal For PayPal

The biggest source of confusion in this deal is why Google would partner with PayPal when it already has Google Pay. To understand that nuance, investors need to dial back to the company’s LT goals set out in the 2025 Investor Day.

At the Investor Day, management explicitly stated that the transaction margin on its platform would accelerate in the form of a step function from >5% growth this year to high single-digit growth next year and eventually accelerate to the LT growth targets of ~10% CAGR.

As things stand, the company looks well set to scale its 2025 targets, i.e., for revenue to grow 4% y/y while transaction margins are expected to outpace revenue growth for the 2nd consecutive time since 2022 at 5.2% y/y.

It’s very important for PayPal to demonstrate that transaction margin is outpacing revenue growth, unlike in ‘22 and ‘23, because it shows the unit economics of processing transactions on its platform is again favorable for PayPal, net of all processing costs. Also, per management’s mid- to long-term goals, it is only going to get better from this year.

There were four “strategic imperatives” that were revealed at the Investor Day, and growing Venmo was one of those imperatives, or pillars, key to growth revival in PayPal’s overall TPV growth (transaction processing volume). If investors go back to Exhibit B above, they can see that the TPV growth in Venmo has been a pleasure to see, with TPV growth back to ~10% growth rates in CY24 at the growth pace of consolidated TPV growth.

Also, as per the Q2 CY25 report, Venmo has accelerated faster since CY24, now growing at 12% y/y and accounting for 18% of PayPal’s TPV, up from the 10% growth rate and 17% TPV share Venmo accounted for in CY24. These updates from Venmo are setting up PayPal beautifully to achieve 40% CAGR through 2027.

For Venmo to achieve 40% TPV growth rates in the next 2-3 years could be termed by some market participants as ambitious for PayPal. But management has clearly seen demand signals to successfully re-target the younger generation of users who typically have a higher willingness to spend if PayPal’s younger target market are given the tools to spend, especially in newer agentic AI-driven shopping formats such as in-chat commerce or conversational commerce that I alluded to at the start of this post. This can be seen in recent research where at least 54-58% of Gen Z and millennials use AI for shopping.

In a recent interview, Perplexity’s founder and CEO laid out the case for why he believes “shopping is the first step” for users to expect AI agents to help beyond “just giving you [users] answers.”

Note: In addition to Google and PayPal announcing their partnership, PayPal had already signed a similar deal with Perplexity for the AI search engine’s users to buy products on Perplexity’s AI chat platform.

At the Investor Day earlier this year, PayPal revealed that they estimated their Online Payments revenue TAM to be ~$125B while their penetration rates to be ~20%. If PayPal can continue to demonstrate higher TPV growth, especially from the flywheel of growing Venmo adoption and agentic commerce/conversational commerce adoption as well, management has an extremely good chance at grabbing the low-hanging fruit of its “affluent users” and re-accelerating transaction margin growth and market penetration.

PayPal Is Still Very Cheap

PayPal is enjoying the best operational leverage of this decade. Management has done exceedingly well to restore the payments company to an operating leverage, which gives the company a robust advantage to now tackle revenue acceleration and gross margins (which will be achieved by the improving unit economics of its business that I indicated earlier).

Regardless of the superior operational leverage and growth runways ahead, PayPal’s shares still trade at a heavily depressed forward earnings multiple of 13x CY25 EPS that is expected to grow 12% y/y to $5.2 per share.

Since 2022, PayPal has grown its EPS in the 4-5% CAGR range, while PayPal is expected to grow EPS in the low teens this year and next year. Revenue slowdowns to the 4-5% range have depressed the valuation multiple for PayPal, but with growth likely accelerating from here on, I certainly do not see why PayPal should trade at 15x forward earnings versus the 13x forward earnings multiple. This implies a PT of ~15% or a PT of ~$79 per share.

In 2026, PayPal’s valuation set up looks even more interesting with the shares trading at 12x CY26 EPS expected to grow in a similar range. Consensus estimates call for revenues to accelerate by 6% in CY26 which I think is quite likely due to rapid adoption of emerging e-commerce category adoption like conversational commerce. The valuation multiple could expand even more next year implying a strong breakout from the current levels it continues to stay rangebound in.

And I firmly believe, agentic commerce enabled by Venmo will be a strong catalyst for the multi-year breakout.

Risks & Other Things To Note

Since conversational/agentic commerce is an emerging market of the rapid evolution of e-commerce, this is going to be a rapidly contested market. In my view, Shopify (SHOP) was one of the early entrants with partnerships with OpenAI. But PayPal is stepping up the competition with its tie-ups with Perplexity and the recently announced Google partnership. PayPal will need to heavily differentiate on the consumer and the partner side to ramp up the appeal of its payment platform within agentic commerce.

One area investors may get confused with is the conflict of interest between Google Pay and PayPal that I called out earlier in this post.

In the context of in-chat commerce that PayPal is trying to enable, investors should understand that the payments company is offering much more than just a payments platform.

In June this year, PayPal’s management explained how they have developed their Agentic toolkit that facilitates end-to-end shopping and fulfillment all within the chat window with the AI agent:

So everything from access to the catalog, order management, the way you do refunds, the way you do reporting, the things you do with disputes, chargebacks, we're still early in building out all of those tools, but those are the tools you need to enable if you're going to have a kind of interaction that ends in conversational commerce.

This range of features is extremely different from the limited pay functionality that Google Pay offers and must not be confused with PayPal’s agentic AI commerce functionalities.

Takeaway

PayPal’s deal with Alphabet’s Google & the May partnership with Perplexity herald a new era of innovation-led growth for the digital payments giant that is doing many things right in setting itself up for accelerating growth ahead. Additionally, the Google deal also unlocks technological synergies with Google’s GCP.

This is setting up PayPal for a strong run over the next many months with valuation multiples still depressed, and I am reiterating my Buy recommendation for PayPal.

data.title: Intel: Only The Paranoid Survive
data.description:
When thinking about Intel (NASDAQ:INTC), one has to remember that only a handful of firms can remotely claim the kind of royalty, intellectual royalty, as Intel. I’m not talking about super savvy business leaders, smart, sharp, and driven – that, one can find in many firms. At Intel, the culture shapers were top-notch science caliber people, in physics. The kind of individuals that transformed technology and the society through science.

In 1968, a firm called Integrated Electronics was found after Bob Noyce and Gordon Moore quit Fairchild and created their own firm, also joined by Andy Grove. The first was the integrated circuit co-inventor and known as the “mayor of the Silicon Valley”. The second is the creator of “Moore’s Law”, in which the number of transistors will double every two years, increasing computing capacity and reducing the cost per transistor. The last one, an immigrant that fled his country after the WWII and, later, the soviet regime, became president of the firm. Know by his belligerent temper, he is the author of “Only the Paranoid Survive”.

The firm started its business with a revolutionary product, a DRAM chip, a dynamic random access memory microprocessor, built after developments made by an engineer called Rober Dennard, from IBM (IBM). This product was perceived by the company as subjected to the “Moore’s Law” and characterized as not specialized depending on the end use, giving Intel the possibility of achieving a competitive edge in a product that could be produced in scale with a growing demand. Later the company succeeded in launching the 4004 chips, the first microprocessor of the world. But it was only in 1980 that Intel closed a contract with IBM to create the personal computer, launched in 1981 with a price of US$ 1,565. The machine utilized a CPU Intel and an operating system developed by a young programmer named Bill Gates. As a second firm, Compaq, launched its PC, the high cost of developing a CPU and Operating System lead to a supply contract with Intel, resulting in a virtual monopoly in the new market, that later became the standard for the X86 technology.

Around this time, Grove established a way of managing based on strict organizational discipline, that lead to the implementation of a detailed production method, that allowed the company to elevate quality standards in all its factories, and also a sense of urgency in the development of new products that could generate competitive edge to Intel. This is the cultural background that seemed to be lost in the last decade, and that we will touch on this article.

In my research, I compare several target firms that are selected based on an appropriate investment thesis. Right now, some of the relevant Theses include Artificial Intelligence Hardware and Applications, Infrastructure and Cybersecurity, and Defense among others. Some comparisons in this article will be made against this broad group, and some will be made with direct competitors. Despite Intel being classified as a CPU player, the comparisons will be made against GPU producers, Nvidia (NVDA) and AMD (AMD). Firms are compared in terms of four elements. Initially, the Cash Discipline is evaluated in terms of how well the operation converts cash. The second is related to the total yield an investor receives considering the pay-out and the potential reinvestment of cash at current prices. An analysis of growth components is the next category, in order to understand commercial success and how it translates into real profitability growth. The last one is a measure of momentum, as it is a strong performance indicator and also may impose important demand engine due to the prevalence of passive investments.

Personal Note: the information on this session is from the wonderful book Chip War: The Fight for the World’s Most Critical Technology, by Chris Miller. My edition is the French one from 2024. The history of the companies and the microprocessor business is fascinating.

What is Intel Selling Today

Intel operates with three segments. The majority of external revenue is booked under the Products segment, while the revenue of other businesses is mainly composed of internal rebooking processes. These are the current business highlights, based on the firm’s latest report:

  • Products: comprises of the Client Computing and the Data Center groups. In the first case, all products related to personal computing are included. Products such PC CPUs, Graphics, Motherboards and Connectivity are part of the items included in the category. On the last, the focus is on Data Center and includes server CPUs, GPUs, ASICs and network infra. In total, this segment represented around 92% of Intel in the six months ended in June/2025, with 23.8% operating margin. Both sales and margins are declining at 2% and 1.6 p.p., respectively. This trend is concentrated in the Client Computing sub-segment while Data Center and Ai is growing at a 5.7% pace with concomitant margin expansion. In both sub-segments, an underperformance is noted. In the CPU market, for example, there was a market growth from 2024 to 2025, indicating that Intel lost some market share. In the Data Center and AI business, the recorded growth is very well below the AI-driven market, indicating that the firm is struggling to establish a position on the fast-paced business. At this point, the Client Computing sub-segment represents around 66% of the total segment.
  • Foundry: consists of intersegment revenues for the production of wafer and substrates fabrication, as well as services provided to other Intel businesses. In the last six months, the revenue represented 36% of Intel, growing 5% YoY. Operating margin of the segment is negative, at the level of -60.4% in line with the measure in the year before. The foundry business is at the core of a transaction undergone by the US government, in which a 10% equity stake was acquired in order to assure that a local production is to be continued, in line with the administration goals towards industrial reshoring and to avoid increased foreign participation in the company. In terms of competition, Intel represents only 6% of the foundry market, including the production of its own chips while Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) is the undisputed leader with 38% and a A+ profitability grade. This advantage seems to be anchored on a clear technical advantage, Intel plans to catch up with the 18A process. The issue is that Intel has demonstrated in the last decade a lack of capacity to execute on the plans and remain competitive in the production, due to the reduced scale that prevents continuous improvement, repetitive delays in 10mn and 7mn architectures, as well as the implementation of extreme ultraviolet lithography, that allows for smaller transistors, all embedded in a culture of secrecy, that led to a low collaboration environment that culminated with product and production shortcomings. Intel now has issues serious challenges in terms of keeping customer trust, as seen in the case of Apple and Microsoft.
  • Other Businesses: representing around 8% of total, with YoY growth of 30% and touching operating profitability of 8% (from negative field one year before), this segment incorporates Altera, a subsidiary of reconfigurable chips used in telecom and data centers in divestment process and Mobileye, a firm on the autonomous driving technology, acquired in 2017, also in divestiture process.

Intel needs a complete turnaround to become profitable. The next session deep dive on its financials and the implications.

Latest Financials

On a historical perspective focused on Net Sales and Gross Margins, the difficulties faced by Intel become apparent. On Figure 1, it is noticeable that both measures declined consistently after 2022. This trend includes the underperformance in the end of 2024, pushed by higher factory start-up costs in Intel 4 and 3 and 18A, Gaudi inventory recognition, and impairment charges. In addition, this effect directly affected the margin performance, as depicted in Figure 2. In the last quarter, an increase in restructuring charges led net income to the negative zone again, highlighting the issues in the operation.

The challenges faced by Intel is even more clear when the firm is compared with the most relevant GPU players, Nvidia and AMD (that is also a traditional competitor in the CPU arena). Figure 3 illustrates the size of the gap between Intel and Nvidia, mostly, but also with AMD. Intel was not able to capture any of the AI investment neither to keep the pace on the CPU market. As a result, the same gap appears in the comparison with the same players. In a previous article, I claimed that the Nvidia succeeded in obtaining strong tailwind from pricing power. Clearly, that was the opposite for Intel, which consistently recorded declining bottom line to an evident underperformance, as shown in Figure 4.

Following the deterioration in the P&L, also the balance sheet depreciated, both in terms of liquidity and solvency. On Figure 5, the Current Ratio depicts the reduction in the working capital position as a result of cash fragility and increasing short-term debt. Total debt, is also the driver of an increase in the Debt / Equity ratio. As the transaction with the US government gets into the books, investors can expect a improvement in balance sheet positions. In terms of liquidity, investors may expect an improvement in Current Ratio as the reflect of the transaction on cash. Also on solvency, the transaction is expected to increase the equity, as 433 million shares will be issued to the US government. In practical terms, the transaction seems like a bridge funding for a struggling company that still has its challenges to overcome. In addition to that capital dilution may impose new burdens on the cash flow claim in each stock.

As a result, Figure 7 depicts cash generation capacity on a four-year analysis, scaled by last year Net Sales. Intel generates negative Free Cash Flow as a result of the aggressive Capex expending is 75% above the operating cash. In this way the firm has a constant funding need, generally realized through debt issuance, or capital injection, as observed in the current quarter. Even though the firm suspended dividends in the end of 2024, in the analyzed horizon there were some dividend payout.

As a result, on the model used in the research, Intel has a weak performance on cash generation, in true shareholder yield and in the growth measures. Figures 8 and 9 illustrate this fact. On the first, a negative yield and a relative high debt level compared to the firms under research localize Intel among the low performers in the group. The other chart clarifies the weak growth measures compared to the group in all categories analyzed. The only indicator that indicates some positive news is related to momentum, as characterized on Figure 10. The price action during this year was pretty much defined by the fact that Intel is recognized as a winner in a policy scenario that pushes for domestic production.

Conclusion

Intel was once characterized as an aggressive player in the semiconductor arena. Eventually, the firm lost its competitive grip in terms of product development and market positioning. In recent years, several missteps were observed in product development and production capabilities, eroding the price power the company had in the CPU market and preventing the firm to position on the new markets, in the way the company succeeded to do when it moved to the CPU arena, avoiding the margin erosion from Japanese competition in the memory market. In the case of GPUs, the firm could not envision the transformation of graphics processors into this powerful revenue engine. The investment by the US government is a bridge but not a solution to the long-term issues the company have. Intel will only be able to justify equity portfolio allocations when a turnaround in clear on the landscape and the company is able to healthy generate cash. Current localization efforts are positive for the company but may not be enough if the foundry efficiency compared to TSMC is not at least comparable.

data.title: The Fed Will Cut Rates Again, And Again
data.description:
In a widely expected development, the Federal Open Markets Committee [FOMC] slashed the target range of the federal funds rate by 25 basis points [bps] to 4-4.25% yesterday. Recent weakness in the labor market was expected to weigh on the committee's mind, and that's exactly what happened. In fact, the concern is significant enough to merit more rate cuts in the remainder of 2025.

Here I unpack yesterday's statement in the context of the current macroeconomic conditions, followed by arguments for why more cuts are in store. Finally, what lower rates mean for investing in equities means right now is discussed.

Labor market concerns dominate

The last two labor market reports have been disappointing, with the establishment survey pointing to far smaller job creation in recent months than had been initially expected. The unemployment rate has risen a bit too. With this as the background, there's not one or two, but five instances in the two paragraphs of economic evaluation in the FOMC statement where it reflects concern on the labor markets. These are as follows:

  • Reflection on economic activity

Much like in the July statement, the Fed continues to maintain that "growth of economic activity moderated in the first half of the year". This is despite the upward revision in GDP growth rate in Q2 2025 to 3.3% from the initial estimate of 3% in the interim. Clearly, the Fed has more than just the GDP numbers on its mind, and that's the labor market as it makes clear in the following remarks.

  • Reference to non-farm payrolls

With revisions to changes in non-farm payrolls being especially weak in the past two releases, the Fed's focus on the indicator was likely. To this extent, the statement has added a remark on it, saying "Job gains have slowed...".

  • Acknowledging rise in unemployment rate

Next, the FOMC statement acknowledges some rise in the unemployment rate. It says that "the unemployment rate has edged up but remains low.". This is a change from the July statement, when it had only said "The unemployment rate remains low...".

  • Missing phrase on labor market strength

Next, conspicuous from its absence is the Fed's previous remark on labor market strength. In July, it had said that "labor market conditions remain solid.". Not so, anymore.

  • Risks weighed towards the labor market

Finally, the committee adds the standard statement "The Committee is attentive to the risks to both sides of its dual mandate..." with a difference. This time, it says that "downside risks to employment have risen," a remark which wasn't there in July.

Note that it's not as if the FOMC isn't concerned about inflation. After all, CPI inflation came in at its hottest levels in seven months in August. It says "Inflation has moved up and remains somewhat elevated" compared with the remark in July that " Inflation remains somewhat elevated." But clearly, it's not as big a concern as the weakness in the real economy is.

Two more rate cuts likely in 2025

With the much speculated rate cut now done and dusted, the question is, are more rate cuts in store? The answer is yes. To understand it, we don't need to look any farther than the Fed's latest economic projections, which were also released with the FOMC statement yesterday. Here are the highlights:

  • Inflation outlook unchanged

To start with, despite the recent uptick in the pace of price rise, the outlook for both PCE and core-PCE based inflation has remained unchanged from the June forecasts at 3% and 3.1% respectively. This explains the Fed's contained concerns about inflation in the latest statement, especially as both figures are at sub-3% levels as per the latest data.

  • Unemployment rate to inch up further

The projection for unemployment rate also remains unchanged at 4.5%, which indicates that the Fed continues to expect the jobs market to deteriorate further. For context, the number came in at 4.3% in August.

  • Lower Fed funds rate seen

But the icing on the cake is the reduction in Fed Funds rates projection to 3.6% from the earlier expectation of 3.9%. For context, until the day before, the effective fed funds rate stood at 4.33% and even after the rate cut, the Fed's policy rate range is higher than 4%. This indicates that some 50bps of rate cuts are in order. With the Fed's preference for 25bps rate cuts, it's then likely that these cuts will happen over the next two meetings in October and December.

25bps rate reduction expected in October

The likelihood of another 25bps rate cut in October is further backed by the probabilities driven by the Fed funds futures' rates. They put an overwhelming probability of 87.7% on a 25bps rate cut in the next meeting. It's worth noting that the probability has jumped from less than 50% a month ago to closer to 90% as of yesterday.

Stock market response

Presumably, as the rate cut was already priced in, the key stock market indices barely moved in yesterday's session. But it does need to be noted that the rate sensitive Dow Jones Industrial Average Index (DJI) was the only one to rise, albeit modestly, by just 0.5%. The possibility of the rate cut serving as a positive catalyst for the index was there, as I discussed in my recent article, even though it wasn't a sure thing.

In comparison to the DJI's rise, the NASDAQ 100-Index (NDX), (NASDAQ:QQQ) and the S&P 500 (SP500, SPX, NYSEARCA:SPY) were down by 0.2% and 0.1% respectively. It can be said that the two indices didn't respond at all, though.

A spectrum of investing opportunities

A look at the sector-wise response to the rate cut reveals that financial services were the most positively impacted by the development (see chart below). This suggests that the positives from a cut in terms of potential loans' demand can outweigh the loss of interest income from smaller rates.

In this vein, it's worth noting that The Financial Select Sector SPDR® Fund ETF (XLF) gets a buy rating from both Seeking Alpha analysts and also its Quant Rating. This can be a good starting place for investors looking for opportunities in cyclicals. The Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR® Fund ETF (XLY) is another such opportunity, which also gets a buy rating.

With labor market softening expected, though, it's also a good idea to keep it safe. And there are plenty of options available here too. My recent analyses of gold miners indicates that the likes of Agnico Eagle Mines (AEM) and Newmont Corporation (NEM) are still promising stock to buy.

Defensives like healthcare stocks also look good. Since the previous FOMC meeting to now, I've given buy ratings to two of them, AbbVie (ABBV) and Eli Lilly (LLY). Other consumer staples like tobacco stocks Altria (MO) and British American Tobacco (BTI) also look good at the present time.

In conclusion

After the July FOMC meeting, I had noted that the Fed had become less sure of where the economy was going and this meant that a rate cut might or might not happen in September. What a difference a month and a half can make. The rate cut was fully expected and priced in before the meeting and the Fed delivered exactly that.

Two more rate cuts are likely during the remainder of 2025 going by the latest projections and probabilities. But if the recent past tells us anything, it's that anything can change. A robust labor market report or hotter than expected inflation can put a pause to rate cuts once again. Unlikely, but possible.

The underlying point here is that there's still continued uncertainty around the US economy. But where it's at right now, is actually a positive for investors, as both cyclical and defensive stocks could do well in the portfolio, which in turn throws up plenty of investing opportunities.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: On Holding: Valuation At Low Levels While Growth Doesn't Stop
data.description:
Investment thesis

On Holding (NYSE:ONON) continues to report growth thanks to strong international expansion, diversification in its product portfolio and a greater presence in the direct-to-consumer channel, which would be a foundation for growth in the coming years. However, the stock is at a historically low valuation and has fallen more than 30% since its peak in early 2025, which presents a buying opportunity.

International growth kicks in

Note: This is an update to my previous article where I covered the ONON business model in more detail.

A little over a year ago I published an analysis of On Holding, which at the time was beginning to establish itself as a relevant player in the premium running segment. My thesis back then was clear: although ON was small compared to giants like Nike or Adidas, its accelerated growth and ability to maintain high gross margins made it an interesting story.

Today, many things have changed, but what hasn't is the brand's traction. If we look at Google Trends as a popularity thermometer, we see that interest in ON continues to grow globally, with the United States as the main driver, but also with clear signs of expansion in Europe, Asia and the Middle East. This confirms that the brand is gaining cultural relevance, which doesn't surprise me given the fact that you only have to look at people's shoes in your daily life to realize that ON is everywhere. And if you ask people about the brand, they'll usually tell you it was worth the purchase.

This geographic expansion is also reflected in the company's financials. Sales in the Americas remained strong in the first half of 2025, with a 24% growth. However, Asia-Pacific, which includes key countries such as China, Japan, South Korea, Taiwan or Australia, grew by an extraordinary 115% year-over-year. This represents an acceleration of international growth, with this region growing only by 84% in 2024 and 76% in 2023. And the Europe, Middle East and Africa region also contributed with growth of 38.5% in the first half of 2025 compared to 18% in 2024.

The importance of this international growth lies in diversification. For a brand like ON, relying too heavily on the U.S. market could limit its long-term potential, so expanding across regions not only reduces geographic risk but also increases brand resilience, as consumer demand in one market can offset weakness in another. More importantly, success in Asia-Pacific and EMEA shows that ON's premium positioning works globally, not just in its home markets. This means that accelerated growth can be maintained for more years if things go well.

Direct-to-Consumer channel is key

Another important aspect of ON’s evolution is its retail strategy. The company continues to expand its network of flagship stores in cities like Paris, New York and Hong Kong, while also pushing its digital channels. Direct-to-consumer (or DTC) sales already represent more than 41% of total revenue in the first half of 2025 compared to 40.7% in 2024 and 37.5% in 2023, outpacing wholesale growth with 47% sales growth in the first half of 2025 compared to 23% for the wholesale channel.

This is relevant because DTC not only improves margins by cutting out intermediaries, but also strengthens the relationship with customers through controlled brand experiences. It wasn't for nothing that Nike tried to aggressively boost its DTC channel in 2021 to rely on its own stores by cutting relationships with retailers like Amazon, Macy's, Foot Locker or Urban Outfitters, but in its case it pulled back after realizing it couldn't fully replace wholesale partners. ON, on the other hand, seems to be striking a better balance, combining wholesale reach with premium flagships that serve as brand billboards (similar to what we see in luxury groups like Hermès or LVMH) where stores are less about volume and more about showcasing identity and exclusivity, at the same time that it continues to grow in the wholesale channel taking advantage of its relationships with retailers

Another element that reinforces the relevance of the DTC strategy is the company's progress in apparel and accessories. While shoes remain the backbone of the business with 94% of sales, this amount has been reducing very little by little and apparel sales grew from just $47 million in 2022 to more than $100 million in 2024 and are up another 67.5% in the first half of 2025. Accessories, although still very small, doubled in 2025 after a 50% growth in 2024.

These categories increase the possibility of cross-selling and full brand immersion for customers. In other words, every flagship store and every click on the ON app is no longer just an opportunity to sell sneakers, also it's a chance to dress customers head-to-toe, which if we see the margins of brands like Lululemon we can realize that it is something very profitable.

Valuation

After a strong start to the year, the company says it expects to end the year with at least CHF 2.91 billion in sales, which would represent a growth of almost 30%. This is very conservative considering that the first half of the year saw a 37.5% growth and the second half is usually the best, as it includes Thanksgiving, Black Friday and Christmas shopping.

So for this year, I think 35% growth or more is possible and then I estimate that the average annual growth for the coming years would be 25%. If we add a gradual expansion of margins as the company stops investing so much in SG&A expenses and improves its operational efficiency, then it could be earning just over CHF 1 billion in net profit in five years. The industry average is a P/E of 20 times, but as a company with untapped growth, above-average margins and a premium brand, I suppose 25 times would be logical too.

So a market cap of $26 billion divided by the current 332 million shares oustanding represents a price per share of $80 or an upside of just under 90% from current price.

While it's not the biggest bargain out there, the fact that valuation multiples are at rock-bottom levels strikes me as an opportunity right now. But, as I said, it's not the cheapest company you can find.

Risks

One of the main risks for ON is that its products belong to the consumer discretionary sector, and recently even premium brands are being affected by the fact that they can easily be postponed in an adverse economic environment. Ultimately, that "these new sneakers can wait" mentality has reached us all when our pocket is a bit tight. And the most recent reports from the retail sector by Jones Lang LaSalle reflect warning signs, such as the fact that 2024 saw the highest level of U.S. bankruptcy filings since 2010 and retail net absorption in 2025 showed 14.5 million square feet in the first half of the year, that is, many more stores are closing than opening.

And despite increased retail spending, consumer sentiment fell in Q1 2025 to its lowest level since 2022, reflecting economic and political uncertainty, which could end up impacting ON sales in the short-term.

Competition also represents a structural risk for ON. The sportswear market is dominated by giants like Nike, Adidas or Puma, who have been top of mind for decades among consumers looking for activewear. This means that ON is fighting for consumer attention, shelf space and digital visibility in an increasingly crowded market. The risk is that if ON's image begins to become outdated or consumers begin to notice that its prices are already too high, the company's differentiation could narrow quickly and the loss of customers could be abrupt, leading to cases like the recent declines of Lululemon and Nike.

Being a premium retail brand is fun, until it stops being one, sales stop growing, margins shrink and your stock plummets.

The bottom line

While the retail situation is currently delicate, ON has demonstrated continued growth and diversification across geographies, sales channels and products, which adds resilience to the business and allows for high-growth rates for many years to come.

Considering the valuation is at rock-bottom levels and although I don't expect it to be one of the top-performing stocks in the coming years, I do think there's value in the current price, especially for a brand that has demonstrated a strong value proposition for the consumer, which is why it seems like a good entry point with a 5 to 10 year time horizon.

data.title: Rivian: The New Georgia Plant Is A Game Changer
data.description:
Updating My Bullish Take On Rivian

My coverage history of Rivian Automotive (NASDAQ:RIVN) stock has seen both bearish and bullish ratings, with the most recent article, which came out in March 2025, suggesting a likely reversal in RIVN stock in the medium term. And it seems to be playing out almost the way I expected back then, with RIVN gaining ~8.58%, although it's far from the S&P 500 index's (SPY) gain of over 16% for the same time.

Now I see that my bullish thesis has only strengthened 2 days ago, when Rivian announced the addition of their new manufacturing facility in Georgia that "will eventually produce the midsize, 5-seater R2 SUV, and R3 crossover", according to the statement. I think that the official groundbreaking should help Rivian stock offset the macro uncertainties in the niche and give it a valuation boost throughout 2H 2025, and probably further into the next year. The narrative should finally get a boost because the new plant only increases Rivian's chances for further market share gains in the US, and it spells "more interested stock buyers" to me. This is why I decided to leave my "Buy" rating on RIVN unchanged for now.

Why Do I Think So?

Looking at Rivian's Q2 results, we see that it looks pretty "ugly" on the surface, because the firm missed on the bottom line by over 25%, according to Seeking Alpha, and their positive beat on the top line was less than 1%, so it looked insufficient to offset the former headline figure's pessimism. But in reality, the stock didn't fall that much after Q2 came out:

Moreover, Rivian even surged by over 13% since August 4th (the day before they released earnings), so the market seemingly didn't view the Q2 EPS miss as something tragic.

If we think about the reasons for the Q2 miss, we'll find that the main thing that drove this was the drop in production: Rivian delivered only 5,979 vehicles for the quarter, which was almost half of the 10,661 vehicles in Q2 2024. Why? The management said on the Q2 earnings call that the real cause for this wasn't the demand slump as it might seem at first glance - it was all about the supply chain-related issues and only partially shifts in trade policy.

We saw a significant decrease in production volume compared to the first quarter as a result of a variety of supply chain-related complexities partially driven by shifts in trade policy.

So, with the operating leverage in place, which tends to work in both directions, Rivian's bottom line was hit the hardest, and that's why the management had no choice but to lower the FY2025 EBITDA loss projections to the new $2-2.25 billion range.

But it doesn't address the main question, "Why didn't the stock dip further since this ugly Q2 print?"

The market is (almost) always a forward-looking mechanism that can price any unprofitable entity well above its high-ROE peer, solely based on future projections. So, in this particular case, Wall Street didn't rely on Rivian's Q2 as if it were a litmus paper. Instead, it decided to focus on what can bring in a massive advantage over other EV peers in the niche, and it is definitely their Software business, which showed ~$376 million in revenue for Q2 at a healthy $129 million gross profit. Nearly half of that revenue came from Rivian's JV with Volkswagen, and it's likely to be just the beginning of real monetization going forward because I think more brands will be looking for joint venture opportunities with Rivian as the latter scales further.

Another thing that we heard on the Q2 call was the updates on Rivian's upcoming R2 platform. We saw obviously that R1 was a groundbreaking product, which gained a lot of buyers' attention, but it was just too complex in manufacturing/assembling, so the firm couldn't quickly scale production and make a profit out of it. Now, with the new R2, which was initially designed to be more cost-efficient and easier to build, Rivian plans to launch the R2 from the existing Illinois facility, and I think it's going to benefit from the shared fixed-cost absorption.

The production volume output of Q2 going from in Q1, around 14,000 units to Q2 being around 6,000 units. The lack of fixed cost absorption, you can really see it in the numbers. And as we said, that was reflective of a lot of the supply chain environment that we're in and some of the trade-related and export control related items that we encountered in Q2, but an important point to call out here is that as we launch R2, the benefits of fixed cost absorption, which will be felt not just by R2, but carrying that across R1, we'll also start to see. And this is really important for us as we grow volume in the plant in our Normal, Illinois production facility going into 2026.

But Q2 data and commentary have been known to the market for over a month now, and it's not the main pillar of my thesis update to date.

As I noted in the very beginning of this article, about 2 days ago, Rivian announced the official groundbreaking of its new manufacturing plant in Georgia, which is going to "produce up to 400,000 vehicles annually by 2028 and employ 7,500 people" (once it's complete, of course).

I call it a game-changer for Rivian's growth story because it helps them shift from a niche EV SUV maker to a mass-market one. If we think about it for a while, their Illinois plant, even at the full capacity of 215,000 units, was never supposed to be enough to satisfy the growing SUV demand in the EV space. As legacy automakers such as General Motors (GM) and Ford (F) are now stepping back on their EV businesses, Rivian is clearly doubling down (which is obvious, because RIVN has the needed expertise). And now, with the new plant in Georgia, Rivian is going to have better scalability prospects over the next few years, and I believe it's exactly what's needed for a narrative change on the forward-looking Street.

The immediate effect on the news was bleak, I agree, but we already see the EPS positive revisions since the Q2 release date, and I think that the Georgian plant can further accelerate this once new updates from analysts covering the stock start to come out.

The stock price is set to reprice higher as a result of this narrative change, in theory, and its sales-based valuation on a forward basis lags Tesla's (TSLA) ratio by a factor of 5 or so:

I know that I'm comparing apples to oranges in a way when I try to match Tesla and Rivian, but for both of them, the EV business remains a core one. And unlike Tesla, which already had its EV glory days, Rivian's story is only getting started:

Over the next 5 years, Rivian's sales are projected to grow with a CAGR of 47.85%, while Tesla's sales are going to expand by only 26% annually for the same period. So, Rivian's P/S of 2.4x looks way more tempting for growth investors, in my view, and the premium versus Chinese EV brands that exist looks reasonable to me as well.

All Rivian needs to keep reversing higher is a confirmation of the narrative shift, which I expect to come throughout 2H 2025 with more upward-sloping earnings revisions. That's why I remain bullish on RIVN, at least for the medium term.

Where Can I Be Wrong?

The main risk to my thesis is that Rivian is going to stay with a net loss for the next few years, and we saw in the past that the break-even consensus projections tend to get lower. So, there's a risk that what we see today in Rivian's consensus EPS estimates is off by a mile, and the firm will stay with deep losses for longer than currently expected.

Rivian has over $7 billion on its balance sheet, and this fact makes the firm quite well-financed for its expansion plans. But there's a risk of further dilution ahead, and if that dilution happens to be larger than bulls may expect, the current shareholders' long positions are going to take a hit.

Also, I may be plain wrong regarding RIVN's valuation. I argued that the price investors pay for Rivian today is reasonable, but I compared its multiples with Tesla's. The comparison sample is just too narrow. And if we compare RIVN with LI and Xpeng (XPEV), we'll see it overvalued.

Final Words

Despite the risk factors, I still believe that I did the right thing when I decided to upgrade Rivian stock to "Buy" in March 2025. Rivian's underperformance relative to the broader market doesn't look dramatic, and while the firm missed on its bottom line for Q2, the loss per share improved by a factor of 2 since 2022, so the trend is generally positive. Anyway, with the latest news around Rivian's Georgian plant, I believe the market's narrative regarding the firm's future should shift eventually with more positive earnings revisions ahead. With that in mind, I expect the stock's price to adjust higher, and it makes my stay bullish on RIVN for now.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: I Lost Up To 90%: My Worst REIT Investments
data.description:
I have been actively investing in REITs (VNQ) for well over a decade, and I have lost a lot of money along the way.

Anyone who pretends to only pick winners is either a liar or has never taken chances.

Investing is a constant learning journey, and some of the lessons have to be learned the hard way.

In today’s article, I am going to give you the chance to learn from my costly mistakes so that you can avoid the same costly losses and headaches.

CBL & Associates Properties, Inc. (CBL)

This is my biggest REIT investing failure ever. I lost about 90% of my capital, and the worst part is that this mistake could have been easily avoided.

In short, CBL is a mall REIT that traded at just around 4x FFO and an estimated 50% discount to its net asset value back when I invested in it nearly 10 years ago. It was also offering a 10%+ dividend yield that appeared well-covered with a low 50% payout ratio.

Back then, I thought that it was so cheap because the market had overreacted to the narrative that e-commerce would kill all malls. We, of course, agreed that this was a headwind, but we thought that this would primarily impact lower-quality malls, leaving the Class B and A segments mostly intact. CBL’s malls weren’t exceptional, but they were often the dominant properties in their secondary markets, and we thought that they would be fine long-term, especially as they add more services, entertainment, and other non-retail uses to their properties.

We were correct about that. Today, its malls are generating higher sales per square foot than ever.

But where we messed up our analysis was the balance sheet. Looking at the surface figures like LTV and Debt-to-EBITDA, it seemed that the company was fine.

But those metrics failed to take the growing capex needs into account. Suddenly, the company had to use a growing chunk of its cash flow to reinvest in its properties just to keep them competitive. Therefore, its actual cash flow available to service the debt and pay the dividend was a lot lower than we thought.

This first led to a dividend cut, and later, as the world shut down due to the pandemic, the company was pushed into bankruptcy.

I lost almost everything. Not all of it because I had switched to the preferred equity, which received a piece of the new common equity after it emerged from bankruptcy.

The lesson here is not to overlook capex and how it can radically change over time.

I think that it is a particularly important lesson for office REITs today. They seem cheap and conservatively leveraged if you look at surface figures. But if the capex surges in the coming years due to the severe oversupply, then the cash flow will get drained, and balance sheets will quickly become stretched.

This recently prompted BXP (BXP) to cut its dividend, despite having one of the strongest balance sheets in the sector. I think that this is a clear red flag of more pain to come.

Medical Properties Trust, Inc. (MPW)

I still own a small position in MPW, so I have not lost all hope, but I have been wrong so far and may be wrong again.

When I first invested in the company, it had an exceptional track record, having massively outperformed the broader market (SPY) up until then:

It had a unique investment strategy, buying hospitals with strong triple net leases, including CPI-based rent escalations, and earning large spreads over its cost of capital. Its properties also enjoyed high rent coverage ratios, which seemed to suggest that its tenants were profitable and unlikely to default on their leases. The management also had a good track record at handling occasional problem tenants, managing to release the properties to other operators.

It then became the target of short sellers who claimed that MPW’s biggest tenant was a lot weaker than what MPW was portraying.

My mistake here was to quickly dismiss the short sellers. Back then, my investment in MPW had been very successful, earning me very large returns. MPW’s management also had a great track record; they had significant equity invested in the company, and they assured us that their tenants were fine, pointing to their solid rent coverage ratios. Finally, I had also recently experienced a short attack targeting another REIT called Farmland Partners (FPI), which was proven to have been criminal in court. All of this led me to dismiss the short sellers, which was a big mistake in hindsight, as they proved to be correct.

Later, its biggest tenant, Steward, ended up filing for bankruptcy, which, coupled with the surge in interest rates, caused its share price to crash. The company became overleveraged with a high risk of an eventual default:

To be fair to MPW’s management, the pandemic black swan, of course, didn’t help either. It caused great pain to hospitals as it delayed a lot of elective surgeries and treatments, and significantly increased healthcare labor costs.

The lesson here is to take short sellers seriously. They are often highly sophisticated investors, and if they are taking the risk of betting against a particular company, it typically means that they have done very deep research.

MPW is now a very different story, as it has turned into a deep value recovery play that depends on future rate cuts and its ability to release properties. Some short sellers have closed their positions. Others continue to expect further downside.

Invesque Inc. (IVQ.U:CA)

Invesque is not officially structured as a REIT, but it is a listed landlord and essentially functions as if it were one.

Back when I first invested in it, the thesis was simple. It was trading at a far lower valuation and higher yield than its senior housing REIT peers Welltower (WELL) and Ventas (VTR). The lower valuation was due to it being primarily listed in Canada and not being structured as a REIT. The management had a great track record, having sold their previous REIT for large gains.

The downside was that it was more heavily leveraged with a 55% LTV, which increased risks. We thought that this was still fine and that the company would reach a higher valuation as it gradually improved its balance sheet.

But instead, the pandemic came shortly after, which caused great pain to senior housing communities.

It caused Invesque to become overleveraged and forced it to start selling assets to pay off debt, greatly reducing its cash flow and putting it at high risk of bankruptcy.

The lesson here is that nothing else matters if the balance sheet isn’t strong enough for the company to survive a black swan. There is no point in earning good returns for a few years if you then lose it all as the company becomes overleveraged and is forced to aggressively sell assets just to survive.

Closing Note

The common theme of my investing mistakes in the REIT sector is excessive leverage. Most of these REITs could have been fine, even despite their setbacks, had they had a strong balance sheet to absorb the shocks.

But their managers were too greedy, took on too much leverage, and paid the price.

As a shareholder, I was too greedy, seeking high yields at the expense of significant risk.

Lesson learned.

At High Yield Landlord, we generally stay away from such speculative deep value plays these days and focus primarily on higher-quality REITs facing temporary issues instead. Those have been our most successful investments over time.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Precious Metals Stocks Are Back With A Vengeance In 2025
data.description:
By Amee Shah Upadhyaya CFA, CA, Senior Analyst - Index Product Support

Precious metals stocks have roared back in 2025 after years of underperformance, fueled by geopolitical tensions and economic uncertainty. The FTSE Global All Cap Precious Metals and Mining Index is up 86% YTD, massively outperforming global equities. Investors are turning to this asset class for its hedging power, diversification benefits, and resilience against inflation.

After two straight years of underperformance, precious metals-related stocks are back with a vengeance so far in 2025, driven by rising geopolitical tensions and tariff uncertainties.

As at the end of August 2025, the FTSE Global All Cap Precious Metals and Mining Index was up 86% in the year to date, outperforming our flagship global equity benchmark, the FTSE Global All Cap Index, by over 70%.

Performance - Total Return (USD)

This performance reflects rapidly rising interest in precious metals-related stocks such as gold miners. Market participants are increasingly turning to this asset class to serve as a hedge against volatility, improve diversification or increase a portfolio’s resilience to inflation.

What’s in the FTSE Global All Cap Precious Metals and Mining index?

Here are the key characteristics of the FTSE Global All Cap Precious Metals and Mining index, with data as at end-August 2025.

  • The index focuses on exposure to global players in the precious metals and mining industry
  • By precious metals, gold mining dominates the index (with a 91.8% weighting), while exposure to platinum, other metals, diamonds and gemstones takes up the remainder of the index
  • The index comprises 69 stocks, with a top 10 holdings concentration of 65%; however, as of the September 22, 2025 index review, the top 10 weights will come down to about 62% due to the inclusion of additional Canadian constituents, bringing the index constituent total to 92.
  • By country, Canada accounts for nearly half of the index weight (48.7%), followed by the US (17%), South Africa (15%) and Australia (9%).

Country Breakdown

Performance rebounded in 2025

The FTSE Global All Cap Precious Metals and Mining index underperformed its reference benchmark (the FTSE Global All Cap index) in 2023 and 2024, despite healthy gains from the gold price. In the year to end-August 2025, the performance of the FTSE Global All Cap Precious Metals and Mining index has rebounded strongly, with a total return in excess of 70% ahead of the reference benchmark.

Calendar Year Total Returns USD (%)
Index YTD 2025 2024 2023 2022 2021 2020 2019 2018 2017 2016
FTSE Global All Cap Precious Metals and Mining Index 85.72 8.25 3.31 -12.23 -5.65 27.22 48.13 -9.6 10.57 58.2
FTSE Global All Cap Index 14.75 16.83 22.19 -17.87 18.54 16.76 27.09 -9.62 24.38 8.98

Source: FTSE Russell. Data as of August 29, 2025. Past performance is no guarantee of future returns. Please see the end for important legal disclosures.

Annualised Total Return (% USD)

As at end-August, the FTSE Global All Cap Precious Metals and Mining Index had delivered long-term outperformance against the global equity market, delivering a 10-year annualized return of 17.4%, compared to 11.4% for the broader index.

This performance comes with significant tracking difference: the performance of the FTSE Global All Cap Precious Metals and Mining Index diverged from that of the FTSE Global All Cap Index by more than 10% (in absolute terms) in six of the nine years between 2016 and 2024.

This, of course, suggests that the index could deliver significant diversification benefits in a broader equity portfolio.

Precious metals and mining ETF landscape

As of August 2025, 16 US-domiciled precious metals ETFs held $35 billion in assets. After experiencing outflows of $2.3 billion in the first half of 2025, precious metals ETFs have seen inflows of $1.4 billion in the two months ended August 2025.[Note 1]

Time to revisit precious metals?

The dramatic recent rise in precious metals-related stock prices highlights the important role this asset class can play during periods of geopolitical and economic uncertainty.

The hedging qualities, diversification benefits and inflation resilience of these stocks may appeal to a broad range of market participants, including those seeking focused exposure to precious metals and mining companies and those looking at an asset allocation switch within a broader equity portfolio.

data.title: Pzena International Value Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Non-U.S. equity markets posted strong gains in the second quarter, helped by a weaker U.S. dollar, despite headwinds from rising U.S. tariffs and ongoing geopolitical volatility. Growth stocks outpaced value, driven by renewed momentum in the technology sector, and our portfolio underperformed its benchmark.

By sector, only energy detracted from absolute performance, and Alibaba (BABA) saw a partial reversal of its strong Q1 performance, as investors reassessed the implications of rising AI investment and continued macroeconomic weakness in China. Sanofi (SNY) declined after reporting mixed results from its late-stage drug pipeline, and lower global energy prices weighed on Equinor’s (EQNR) share price.

The financial sector was the standout performer, and the top individual contributor, U.K. grocer J Sainsbury (OTCQX:JSNSF), rebounded, as the company continued to execute well amid heightened competitive pressure in the U.K. grocery market. German auto supplier Continental (OTCPK:CTTAY)(OTCPK:CTTAF) benefited from investor optimism around its ongoing corporate restructuring and stronger-than-expected earnings, while Daimler Truck’s (OTCPK:DTRUY) valuation recovered, as concerns over tariff impacts and macroeconomic headwinds subsided throughout the quarter.

In the second quarter, we established new positions in two leading Japanese companies, capitalizing on attractive entry points. Murata (OTCPK:MRAAY) is a global leader in electronic passive components, particularly capacitors and inductors. Concerns related to currency fluctuations and China resulted in an attractive buying opportunity for a business we believe has sustainable cost and technology leadership, with long-term growth potential in EVs and AI-driven data centers. Daikin (OTCPK:DKILY)(OTCPK:DKILF), a global leader in air conditioning systems, is facing macroeconomic uncertainty in several geographies as well as tariff pressures. We believe there is upside potential through margin improvement and a renewed focus on capital efficiency. We added to our positions in Olympus (OTCPK:OLYMY) and Rexel (OTCPK:RXLSF) on relative weakness and trimmed our holdings in Shell (SHEL) and Evonik (OTCPK:EVKIF)(OTCPK:EVKIY).

The relatively uncertain macro environment and questions over tariffs continue to keep the market on edge, but this volatility can create opportunities for long-term value investors.
